Last galaxy's harmonics (16 catalogue fields, peak-normalised) —

Sonification map — catalogue → oscillator inactive

Create independent groups. Inputs and outputs inside a group only influence each other — so you can e.g. drive amplitude from redshift in one group while a second group routes u−g colour to the first five harmonics, without either group contaminating the other's parameters.
How a group computes an output.
• Direct mapping. Each selected (input→output) pair applies an affine rescale from the input's physical range [Flo,Fhi] to the output's native range [plo,phi]:
  P(g) = plo + (F(g)−Flo)/(Fhi−Flo) · (phi−plo), clamped to [plo, phi]. Lossless and reversible.

• Graph mode — scientifically-valid N → M resampling. The group's N inputs (in the chosen order) form a discrete graph y0..N-1. Each raw value is first affine-rescaled into [0,1] using its physical range (lossless). Optional peak-normalise divides by the max (shape preserved exactly). The N-vector is then matched to the group's M outputs with:
  • N = M — direct assignment, no transform.
  • N < M — Piecewise Cubic Hermite Interpolation (PCHIP, Fritsch & Carlson 1980). Monotonicity-preserving, passes exactly through every original sample, zero overshoot — critical for bounded parameters like amplitude [0,1].
  • N > M — Anti-aliased box integration (Nyquist-safe). Each output bin receives the arithmetic mean of the inputs that fall in its interval on t ∈ [0,1]. This is a zero-order low-pass + decimation; it preserves Σy (DC/area) and cancels all components above the output Nyquist limit, so no aliasing is possible.

• Merging groups. Each output is written by at most one group — the earliest group (top-most in the list) that maps that output wins. Unmapped outputs fall back to the oscillator's slider defaults (harmonics fall back to the galaxy's own 16-field fingerprint).

Sound-check · listening calibration not calibrated

A short interactive calibration to protect your hearing and avoid painful loudness spikes. Output is peak-limited to your chosen safe level by a pre-master soft-knee limiter and a hard output-gain cap. Tones follow ITU-R BS.1770-4 digital RMS conventions; the 1 kHz sinusoidal anchor is produced at a known digital level so you can set a comfortable system volume before any galaxy is played.
⚠ Before you start — remove headphones, lower your system volume to roughly 10–20 %, then re-seat the headphones. Do not skip this step; loud, narrow-band spikes can damage hearing.
1
1 kHz reference tone at −20 dBFS RMS (≈ −17 dBFS peak). Hold, then adjust your system volume until the tone is clearly audible but comfortable — a relaxed conversational level.
2
Safe-level cap. Set the maximum peak output that will ever reach your ears. Default −6 dBFS leaves headroom against transients. The pre-master limiter enforces this even if sonification mapping pushes amplitude.
−6.0 dBFS
3
Range screen. Plays a logarithmic frequency sweep across the oscillator's working band (55 → 1760 Hz, 5 s) at the capped level. Stop immediately if anything sounds painful or resonant — lower the cap and re-run.
4
Confirm. Once the reference tone and sweep feel comfortable, confirm to activate the calibration. The level cap persists for the rest of the session.

Dataset · catalogue source —

The viewer consumes catalogue records with the short-key schema documented below. Any JSON array of objects using the same keys (a d z u g r i zz pm p50 p90 ex vd …) renders identically. Load a local .json or .csv (comma-separated, header row required), or pick one of the bundled compatible catalogues.

Bundled / compatible catalogues

Load from your computer

Schema

Required: a (RA°), d (Dec°), z (redshift).
Recommended: u, g, r, i, zz (model mags), pm (petroMag_r), p50, p90 (Petrosian R50, R90, arcsec), ex (A_r extinction), vd, vde (velocity dispersion, σ & err), ha, hb, o3, n2 (emission lines ×10⁻¹⁷ erg s⁻¹ cm⁻²), d4 (Dn4000), bp (BPT), lm (log M*/M☉), P (plate), M (mjd), F (fiberID).
Missing optional fields are handled gracefully (catalogue-field sliders fall back to defaults; sonification inputs map to 0).
—

Accessibility · layout-safe preferences default

All options are strictly bounded so fonts never exceed their container and nothing is pushed off-screen. Font scaling is limited to 0.90×–1.20× of baseline — a safe range validated against every fixed panel in the UI. Settings persist per browser (localStorage if available; session-only otherwise).

Text size

Contrast & motion

Audio accessibility

The pre-master limiter and peak-cap live in Sound-check. For an even quieter session, use the slider below to further attenuate the overall output (on top of the cap, never above it).
0.0 dB

How to · foolproof user guide v1

A step-by-step walk-through of every part of the application. Read top-to-bottom on first use; jump to a section with the chips below afterwards. None of the steps depend on prior knowledge — every concept is defined where it first appears.

1 · What this app is

A scientific viewer + sonifier for an SDSS DR8 sky patch. Each galaxy on the canvas comes from a real spectroscopic measurement; you can render any sub-field, hover for the full catalogue record, plan an observer's "route" through space, and turn that route into sound by mapping galaxy properties to oscillator parameters.
The TL;DR for first-time users: (1) open 🔊 Sound-check and set a comfortable peak cap; (2) hover the canvas to inspect galaxies; (3) open Sonification Settings → Syntax, click Set start then click a galaxy, then Set end then click another, hit Play route; (4) tweak the sound from Phonetics and the property→sound mapping from Semantics.

2 · Field & viewer (top bar — α, δ, size)

The three numeric inputs at the top are the single source of truth for what you're looking at. They define the centre of the field on the celestial sphere and how wide a square it is.
α (alpha) = right ascension, in degrees, 0…360. The sky's "longitude".
δ (delta) = declination, in degrees, −90…+90. The sky's "latitude".
Size = side of the square field of view, in degrees (0.05° to 8°). The bundled catalogue covers 8°×8° centred near α=239.75°, δ=+27.27°.
Press Render to apply, Reset to go back to the default. Hover any galaxy → tooltip + side-panel record. Click → opens the SDSS DR17 Explore page in a new tab.

3 · Camera HUD (bottom-right)

The translucent cluster of buttons in the bottom-right corner is a Google-Earth-style telescope HUD. It is always available; you don't need to open a panel.
Drag the canvas with the mouse — slews the pointing (α, δ change). The drag is corrected by 1/cos δ so on-sky motion matches mouse motion at any latitude.
Mouse wheel on the canvas — zooms the field of view, clamped to [0.05°, 8°].
D-pad — N/S/E/W single-step nudges. The slider sets the "slew rate" in degrees-per-click.
＋ / − — halve / double the field of view.
⤢ — fit the full 8°×8° catalogue.
⌂ — reset to default pointing.
The HUD becomes fully opaque on hover and shows live α, δ, FoV in a popover above it.

4 · 🔊 Sound-check (do this FIRST)

⚠ Always run the Sound-check before any sonified playback. Narrow-band tones at unknown levels can damage hearing.
The signal chain is: oscillator → formant filters → soft-knee limiter → output-gain cap → speakers. The Sound-check controls the limiter+cap, which everything else must pass through.
Step 1 — Reference tone. Play the 1 kHz reference (−20 dBFS RMS / −17 dBFS peak). Adjust your system volume so the tone is clearly audible but comfortable.
Step 2 — Peak cap. Move the slider; the limiter threshold and the hard output cap update live. Default −6 dBFS leaves headroom for transients.
Step 3 — Sweep. Plays a 5-second log sweep 55→1760 Hz. Listen for any frequency that sounds painful or resonant; if so, lower the cap and re-run.
Step 4 — Confirm. Locks in the cap for the session.

4b · 🚨 PANIC — emergency mute

A single, always-visible kill switch for all audio output. Sits in the top bar on the far right, styled red so it is impossible to miss in an emergency.
What it does. Instantly forces the master bus gain to zero. Because the PANIC gate is the last node before the audio destination (chain: oscillator → formants → EQ → limiter → cap → PANIC → speakers), nothing can bypass it — not stuck notes, not graph-mode overlaps, not a runaway preset.
How to trigger. Click the red 🚨 PANIC button, or press Ctrl/⌘ + . from anywhere.
How to restore. Click it a second time; the button label updates to "SOUND MUTED — click to resume" while active so the state is never ambiguous. Restores the previous master level exactly (true toggle).
Accessibility. aria-pressed reflects state; the status strip audio indicator updates to muted.

4c · 🎛 Frequency EQ (optional)

A five-band parametric equaliser on the master bus. Off by default (all bands at 0 dB, so fully transparent). Enable it only if a particular spectral region is uncomfortable on your playback chain, or for hearing-accommodation needs.
Bands. Five peaking biquads at default centres 80, 250, 1 000, 4 000, 12 000 Hz. Each band has independent Freq (log-mapped slider), Gain (−24 … +24 dB), and Q (0.1 … 18) controls, plus a per-band Bypass button that forces the band to 0 dB without removing it from the chain.
Presets. Low-cut gently attenuates sub-bass rumble; Presence + lifts the 4 kHz region for clearer vowel articulation; Soft tames the 1 kHz band for long listening sessions. Reset returns all bands to flat 0 dB.
Signal chain. The EQ sits before the limiter and output cap, so any boost you apply is still bounded by the Sound-check cap you set earlier. The PANIC gate remains the last node.
Open. Top-bar → 🎛 EQ. Closes with Esc.

5 · 📂 Dataset

Switch the active catalogue. The bundled MPA-JHU 8°×8° sample is selected by default. You can also load your own JSON or CSV file.
Required keys per row: a (RA°), d (Dec°), z (redshift).
Recommended keys: u g r i zz (mags), p50 (Petrosian R50″), p90, vd (σ velocity dispersion), … See the schema card inside the Dataset panel.
Missing optional fields are handled gracefully — the corresponding sonification inputs map to 0.

6 · Phonetics — oscillator settings

Open via Sonification Settings → Phonetics. This panel defines the timbral identity of each played note: amplitude, base frequency, ADSR envelope, three formant filters, and a 16-harmonic spectrum. These are the defaults; the Sonification Map can override any of them per-galaxy.
Amp — peak amplitude (post-limiter).
Base freq — fundamental in Hz.
ADSR — Attack, Decay, Sustain, Release. Note duration is automatically capped to the time until the next note in a route, scaled proportionally.
Formants 1-3 — peaking biquads. Frequency in Hz, Boost in dB, Bandwidth in octaves (Q = 1 / (2·sinh(ln2/2·BW))).
Harmonics — 16 sliders building a custom periodic wave. Per-galaxy, these are replaced by the galaxy's own 16-field fingerprint unless overridden by the map.

6b · Click-to-sonify (no route needed)

The fastest way to listen. Works as soon as sonification is active — no route, no planning.
How. Enable at least one mapping in Sonification Settings (any group: Phonetics, Syntax, or Semantics). Then simply click any galaxy on the canvas. You hear one note synthesised from that galaxy's catalogue values through the current mapping.
Note duration. The ♪ click slider in the bottom-left status strip sets how long each click-note lasts (0.05 s – 4 s, default 0.8 s). Your choice is remembered across sessions.
Status feedback. A toast at the top of the window confirms the galaxy name and duration of every triggered note. If sonification is off, the toast tells you what to do instead.
Still want the SDSS link? The SDSS DR17 Explore page for any galaxy is still reachable three ways, so the feature is not lost:
  · Alt-click (or ⌘-click, or Shift-click, or Ctrl-click) a galaxy on the canvas
  · Middle-click the canvas on a galaxy
  · open the right-hand side panel for that galaxy and click the "object summary ↗" link
The hover tooltip also reminds you of both actions.

7 · Syntax — route planner

Open via Sonification Settings → Syntax. The route is the temporal grammar of the sonification: the order in which galaxies are played.
Set start → click a galaxy on the canvas → that galaxy is the start.
Set end → click a galaxy → that's the end.
Algorithm: Dijkstra over a 24-NN graph in 3-D comoving-frame Mpc coordinates ("Optimal"); 6-NN graph ("Sparse"); or greedy nearest-step ("Greedy").
Speed — fraction of the speed of light at which the observer travels. Each arrival triggers one note.
Activate notes — toggle whether the route plays sound.

8 · Semantics — sonification map

Open via Sonification Settings → Semantics. This is where you decide which catalogue properties drive which oscillator parameters. The panel can host multiple independent groups; their outputs are merged by a first-group-wins rule.
Inputs (green chips) — catalogue fields: mag u/g/r/i/z, fluxes, colours, redshift, Sérsic n, R50, R90, concentration, RA, Dec.
Outputs (yellow chips) — every oscillator parameter: amp, freq, envA-D-S-R, fmtF/B/W 1-3, h0…h15.
Modes per group:
  • Graph — N inputs are mapped to M outputs via a resampling curve (PCHIP up, anti-aliased box down).
  • Linear Regression — a supervised ridge model that you train on example rows (see next section).

9 · Linear Regression — supervised mapping

Inside Semantics, switch a group to LR mode. The solver fits W = (XᵀX + λI)⁻¹ XᵀY in closed form. Inputs are auto-normalised to [0,1] using each input's registered range. Outputs are kept in their native parameter units and clamped to range at inference time.
Step 1. Select input chips and output chips (the training table updates live).
Step 2. Click + Add row to create an empty row. You can either type values directly, or use the two helper buttons:
  ⤓ Fill inputs from galaxy — type a galaxy id (catalogue index, 0…N−1) and click; the LAST row's input cells are filled with that galaxy's catalogue values.
  ⤓ Fill outputs from osc — fills the LAST row's output cells from the current Phonetics settings (sliders + harmonics).
Step 3. Set ridge λ (default 1e-3 is safe). Click Train. You'll see per-output R² and MSE.
Step 4. Save the model to .json for later use; the saved file includes weights, input/output ranges, and a stale-model schema so loading checks your selection.
Need at least K+1 rows for K inputs (otherwise XᵀX is singular). If the solver throws "singular system", either add more diverse rows or raise λ.

10 · ⚙ Accessibility

Text size — bounded UI scale (0.90× / 1.00× / 1.10× / 1.20×). Affects only chrome, not the canvas.
High-contrast — black background, white text, bright outlines.
Reduce motion — disables CSS transitions and animations.
Crosshair cursor — easier to track on dark imagery.
Strong focus ring — bright yellow outline on keyboard-focused elements.
Extra attenuation — a 0 to −24 dB shelf on top of the Sound-check cap. Audio can only get quieter, never louder.
Settings persist in localStorage.

11 · Troubleshooting

"No sound." Open Sound-check, raise the cap, click Play 1 kHz. If still silent, your browser may have auto-suspended the AudioContext — any click resumes it.
"Painful resonance at one note." Lower the cap to −12 dBFS and re-run the sweep; reduce the formant boost on F1/F2/F3 in Phonetics.
"Loaded a CSV but nothing rendered." Your file must have a header row with at least a, d, z columns, and the field centre must lie inside the file's coverage.
"Train fails: singular system." You have fewer rows than K+1, or your inputs are linearly dependent. Add diverse rows or raise ridge λ.
"Drag not slewing." Make sure you start the drag on empty sky, not on a galaxy (clicking a galaxy is reserved for the inspector / route picker).

12 · Citations

Catalogue: SDSS DR8 spectroscopic galaxy sample with MPA-JHU value-added products (Aihara et al. 2011, ApJS 193, 29; Brinchmann et al. 2004, MNRAS 351, 1151). Photometry: SDSS model magnitudes, AB system, u g r i z. Redshifts: SDSS Spectro-1D (Bolton et al. 2012), zWarning = 0. Projection: gnomonic tangent plane (Calabretta & Greisen 2002, A&A 395, 1077). Colour: Lupton et al. 2004, PASP 116, 133. Sérsic profile: Sérsic 1968. Routing: Dijkstra 1959, Numer. Math. 1, 269; k-NN graph (Friedman, Bentley & Finkel 1977, ACM TOMS 3, 209). Cosmology: Hogg 1999, astro-ph/9905116. Sonification limiter: ITU-R BS.1770-4.

Frequency EQ 5-band parametric · post-master

Optional 5-band parametric equaliser inserted after the master gain and before the limiter. Bypassed by default (all band gains at 0 dB) — the signal is bit-transparent until you move a slider. Useful for taming a resonant formant or rolling off low rumble on small speakers.
SDSS DR8 sky patch — user-specified field. Each galaxy is placed at its catalogued (α, δ) via a gnomonic (TAN) projection; surface brightness follows a Sérsic profile with Re = R50 and index n inferred from the concentration C = R90/R50; colour is composed either from SDSS g, r, i fluxes with the Lupton et al. (2004) asinh algorithm, or — in SED → sRGB mode — by treating the five u g r i z magnitudes as SED samples, interpolating Fλ across the visible band and convolving with the CIE 1931 2° colour matching functions to obtain a physically-defined perceptual sRGB colour.

Interaction. Hover → full catalogue record (right panel). Click → play a single sonified note for that galaxy (requires sonification to be active; set note length with the ♪ click slider in the bottom-left status strip). Alt/⌘/Ctrl/Shift-click (or middle-click) → open the SDSS DR17 Explore page for that galaxy.
—

Galaxy inspector

Hover any galaxy in the field to load its complete SDSS catalog record here.
No galaxy selected.
Data provenance. Catalog: SDSS DR8 spectroscopic galaxy sample with MPA-JHU value-added products (Aihara et al. 2011, ApJS 193, 29; Brinchmann et al. 2004, MNRAS 351, 1151). Photometry: SDSS model magnitudes, AB system, u g r i z (3551, 4686, 6166, 7480, 8932 Å). Redshifts: SDSS Spectro-1D pipeline (Bolton et al. 2012), zWarning = 0. Projection: gnomonic tangent plane (Calabretta & Greisen 2002, A&A 395, 1077). Colour: Lupton et al. 2004, PASP 116, 133. Light profile: Sérsic 1968; bn from Ciotti & Bertin 1999. Concentration → n mapping: Strateva et al. 2001, AJ 122, 1861.

Embedded sample. This single HTML file ships with an 8° × 8° sub-catalog centred on the densest MPA-JHU region (α = 239.75°, δ = +27.27°) — — spectroscopic galaxies. The renderer accepts any field fully contained within that square.
